title: Quiz: Molecular Geometry
description: Test your understanding of VSEPR theory and molecular geometry prediction

questions.question: What is the molecular geometry of water (H₂O)?
questions.explanation: Water has 4 electron domains (2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs), giving tetrahedral ELECTRON geometry. But with only 2 atoms bonded, the MOLECULAR geometry is bent, with an H-O-H angle of 104.5°.

questions.question: Why is the bond angle in H₂O (104.5°) less than the tetrahedral angle (109.5°)?

options.text: Hydrogen atoms are smaller than other atoms
Lone pairs repel more strongly than bonding pairs
Oxygen is more electronegative than hydrogen

questions.explanation: Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs and repel bonding pairs more strongly. This compresses the H-O-H angle from the ideal 109.5° to 104.5°.

questions.question: What is the molecular geometry of SF₆?
questions.explanation: SF₆ has 6 bonding domains and no lone pairs on sulfur. The 6 fluorine atoms arrange octahedrally around sulfur to minimize repulsion, with 90° angles.

questions.question: In geometry optimization, what quantity is minimized?
questions.explanation: Geometry optimization finds the nuclear arrangement that minimizes the total energy (electronic energy plus nuclear repulsion). At the minimum, all forces (gradients) are zero.

questions.question: Electron geometry and molecular geometry are always the same.
questions.explanation: False! They're only the same when there are no lone pairs. Electron geometry considers ALL electron domains, while molecular geometry describes only the arrangement of ATOMS. For example, water has tetrahedral electron geometry but bent molecular geometry.

questions.question: XeF₂ has 3 lone pairs on xenon. What is its molecular geometry?
questions.explanation: XeF₂ has 5 electron domains (2 bonding + 3 lone pairs), giving trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry. The 3 lone pairs occupy the equatorial positions, leaving the 2 F atoms in axial positions, resulting in a linear molecular geometry.

questions.question: Which describes the gradient (∇E) at an equilibrium geometry?
questions.explanation: At equilibrium geometry, all forces are zero. Since force = -∇E, this means the gradient (first derivatives of energy) equals zero. This is the mathematical definition of a minimum on the potential energy surface.

questions.question: Which molecules have a bent molecular geometry? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: a) H₂O: 4 domains, 2 lone pairs → bent. b) CO₂: 2 domains, 0 lone pairs → linear. c) SO₂: 3 domains, 1 lone pair → bent. d) OF₂: 4 domains, 2 lone pairs → bent (like water).
